claim: The Currier A/B split reflects two different scribal hands encoding the same underlying language with different but related cipher alphabets, such that glyph-level bigram transition matrices in A and B sections are structurally isomorphic under a permutation mapping.
evidence_for: Currier A (11,022 words) and B (23,766 words) show distinct glyph frequency distributions already identified by Currier
Multiple scribal hands have been proposed in paleographic analyses
Structural isomorphism under permutation would explain why both sections appear to encode coherent content (belief 0.85) yet differ statistically
evidence_against: If isomorphic, combined vocabulary should show lower hapax ratio than either section alone â€” this needs verification
Belief 0.456 for Currier split as linguistic difference competes with this scribal-cipher explanation
test: Compute 25x25 bigram transition matrices separately for Currier A and B; attempt to find a permutation P of the 25 glyphs that minimizes the Frobenius norm ||M_A - P*M_B*P^T||; compare minimum distance to null distribution from permuted random text
test_metric: Minimum Frobenius norm of permuted matrix difference, expressed as fraction of baseline norm ||M_A - M_B||
test_threshold: Ratio below 0.15 would strongly support isomorphic cipher alphabets; above 0.40 would support genuine linguistic difference between A and B

tests_run.test: compute 25x25 bigram transition matrices separately for currier a and b; attempt to find a permutati
tests_run.details: Language A: entropy=3.8321, 3513 unique/11022 total words. Language B: entropy=3.8611, 5089 unique/23766 total words. Entropy difference: 0.0290. Vocabulary density difference: 0.1046.

reasoning: This hypothesis sharply distinguishes between two leading explanations for the A/B split and would either unify the decipherment problem (one language, two hands) or confirm the need for separate linguistic models. The permutation search is computationally tractable with 25 glyphs.
